skill.name: zig-best-practices
skill.description: Provides practical Zig guidance for type-first design, explicit errors, memory management, comptime validation, C interop, and debugging patterns.
skill.summary: Apply idiomatic Zig patterns for safer types, clearer errors, allocator-aware code, and focused debugging.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-confidence pattern hits, but review found they are Markdown examples and Zig language constructs, not executable marketplace behavior. No prompt injection text, credential exfiltration, malicious network use, or runnable scripts were found in the reviewed files. The remaining risk is low because the skill includes examples for environment-based configuration and C/system interop that users should apply carefully.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Require Normal Secret Handling
low_findings.description: SKILL.md shows a typed Config struct with DATABASE_URL and API_KEY loaded from environment variables. This is legitimate configuration guidance and does not transmit secrets, but generated applications should avoid logging or exposing these values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show environment variable access for database and API key values. Review found no network transmission or logging of those values, so the risk is limited to normal secret-handling caution.

low_findings.title: C Interop Guidance Can Touch System APIs
low_findings.description: C-INTEROP.md demonstrates importing C headers and declaring a Windows user32 extern function. This is expected for a Zig interop guide, but users should review generated FFI code because C boundaries can reduce memory and API safety.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly teaches C imports and extern declarations. The context is educational Zig code, not an instruction to run hidden commands, so the concern is capability risk rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Backtick Command Detection Is Markdown Formatting
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and Zig code fences. No Ruby or shell backtick execution was found in the reviewed skill files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside Markdown prose or fenced Zig examples. There is no shell script, Ruby source, or command-substitution execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Detection Is Zig @import
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import findings are false positives for Zig compile-time imports such as @import("std"). These examples import Zig modules and do not perform JavaScript dynamic loading.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is Zig @import in fenced Zig examples. It is not JavaScript import(), and it is not executed by the skill marketplace.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation URLs Are Reference Links
dangerous_patterns.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to Zig language documentation and learning resources. They are static references and no code in the skill fetches or posts data to them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines are a References list at the end of SKILL.md. No HTTP client call or data transfer logic appears with the URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Not Supported By Context
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers, but review found no cryptographic implementation or recommendation at the cited contextual lines. No evidence found for malicious or unsafe cryptography guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe the skill, generic queue cleanup, and guide references. They do not contain MD5, SHA-1, custom crypto, or credential hashing guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Combined Execution Network Credential Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combined code execution, URLs, and credential terms from separate documentation examples. Review found no combined flow that executes code, reads credentials, and sends data over a network.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious elements occur in unrelated educational examples and reference links. There is no executable file, no exfiltration endpoint, and no prompt text directing the agent to misuse secrets.

content.value_statement: Zig projects often fail from unclear error contracts, unsafe allocation patterns, and weak type boundaries. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Zig patterns for safer implementation and review.
actual_capabilities: Recommends tagged unions, explicit error sets, and domain-specific types for safer Zig APIs.
Guides allocator use with defer, errdefer, arenas, and std.testing.allocator leak checks.
Explains comptime validation, type-returning generics, and careful anytype usage.
Provides patterns for scoped logging, optional handling, and configuration loading.
Covers C header imports, extern declarations, callbacks, and C type mapping basics.
Shows memory debugging patterns using GeneralPurposeAllocator and testing allocators.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not run Zig builds, tests, or formatters by itself.
Examples may need updates for projects using a different Zig version than the referenced documentation.
C interop advice is introductory and does not replace review of platform-specific ABI details.
Security review of generated application logic remains the user responsibility.

use_cases.title: Review a Zig Module
use_cases.description: Check a module for explicit error sets, allocator ownership, exhaustive switches, and safe optional handling.

use_cases.title: Design a Reusable Container
use_cases.description: Use comptime type parameters and allocator-aware APIs to plan a queue, stack, tree, or domain collection.

use_cases.title: Debug Allocation Problems
use_cases.description: Apply GPA and std.testing.allocator patterns to find leaks, missing deinits, and cleanup gaps in tests.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Zig Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small Zig file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Zig file for the practices in zig-best-practices. Focus on error handling, const usage, allocator flow, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Improve API Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Refine a public API before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help redesign this Zig API using tagged unions, explicit error sets, and domain-specific types. Explain each type choice briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Memory Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Zig allocation path. Suggest defer, errdefer, arena, or std.testing.allocator changes that would make cleanup reliable.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Interop Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Zig wrapper plan for this C API. Include @cImport usage, callback boundaries, pointer safety, error mapping, and test strategy.

output_examples.input: A Zig parser returns anyerror and stores separate loading, data, and error fields.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a specific ParseError set and a tagged union state model, then explains which invalid states are removed.

output_examples.input: A test allocates an ArrayList but sometimes exits early on error.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the missing cleanup path and recommends placing defer or errdefer immediately after resource acquisition.

output_examples.input: A project needs a minimal wrapper around a C callback API.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines header import, C calling convention, opaque pointer casting, error boundary handling, and safety checks.

best_practices: Define data structures, error sets, and function signatures before implementation details.
Pass allocators explicitly and place cleanup next to every successful acquisition.
Use comptime validation and exhaustive switches to catch invalid states early.
anti_patterns: Using anyerror or anytype when a specific error set or comptime type parameter would be clearer.
Scattering environment lookups, allocator choices, or logging scopes across unrelated functions.
Letting C pointer casts, callbacks, or optional unwraps cross boundaries without explicit checks.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Zig code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or host tool must run builds, tests, and formatters.

faq.question: Which Zig topics does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes type-first design, explicit errors, allocator ownership, comptime validation, logging, optionals, C interop, and memory debugging.

faq.question: Can it help with memory leaks?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes GPA, std.testing.allocator, defer, errdefer, arenas, and cleanup placement patterns.

faq.question: Is the C interop advice production complete?
faq.answer: No. It gives starting patterns. Production wrappers still need ABI, ownership, threading, and platform review.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are the security findings blocking?
faq.answer: No. Review found false positives from Markdown examples and low-risk guidance that should be applied with normal code review.
